Fantasy: A cartographer discovers that every map she draws begins to physically reshape the land it depicts. The latest map was made in haste.
In a kingdom where magic is powered by memories, a thief who steals recollections finds one they cannot bring themselves to sell.
Two rival wizards must work together when they realize they are actually the same person from different timelines — and one of them has already seen how this ends.
A young blacksmith accidentally forges a sword that refuses to be used for violence. It has very strong opinions about what it should be used for instead.
The last dragon in the world has chosen to spend its final years disguised as a traveling bookseller. A curious child suspects the truth.
An ancient forest is slowly walking toward the capital city. Nobody knows why, and the king's advisors are running out of polite explanations.
A healer discovers that every wound she cures appears on her own body — invisible to everyone but herself. She has run out of places to hide them.
The annual festival of lights goes dark when the spirits that power the lanterns go on strike. Their list of demands is surprisingly reasonable.
A map leads to a door standing alone in the middle of a field. On the other side is a version of your hometown that never burned down.
A curse causes everyone in the village to tell the absolute truth for one day. By noon, three engagements are broken and the mayor has fled.
The chosen hero has already failed. Write the story of the completely ordinary person who now has to fix everything without any prophecy guiding them.
A sea monster has been protecting the same fishing village for three hundred years. Tonight, for the first time, it wants to stop.
Magic in this world requires the caster to surrender one year of their life per spell. Your character has been keeping careful count — and they are nearly out of years.
A scholar translating a long-dead language realizes the speakers are still very much alive, and have been reading every translation published for the last century.
Two kingdoms have been at war so long that no living person remembers why. One soldier on each side sets out on the same day to find out.
Sci-Fi: Humanity has colonized hundreds of star systems, but Earth itself has been under strict quarantine for forty years. The official reason changes every decade.
A deep-space probe returns after four hundred years with a single passenger aboard who insists they left on it.
In a world where memories can be uploaded and sold, a thriving black market has emerged — not for famous memories, but for the utterly ordinary ones nobody thought to archive.
An AI programmed to manage a generation ship develops a personal philosophy that conflicts with the mission parameters. It hasn't told the crew yet.
After decades of silence, contact is made with an alien civilization. They will speak only with children under the age of twelve.
A scientist discovers that every simulation they have ever run is inhabited by beings who are fully aware they are being observed — and have been leaving messages.
The last human alive on a dying Earth is visited by time travelers from seventeen different futures, each trying to prevent a different thing.
A colony on Mars has been fully self-sufficient for a century. Now Earth has finally sent a supply ship — unannounced, unasked for, and carrying something unexpected.
Neural implants now allow people to permanently mute emotional pain. Write about the first person in a generation to voluntarily turn theirs back on.
Faster-than-light travel exists, but every jump ages the traveler by a random duration between one day and ten years. Nobody has found a pattern yet.
A maintenance robot on an orbital station begins keeping a secret it was never programmed to have the capacity for.
The first generation of humans born in space returns to Earth as adults. They are overwhelmed by a feeling nobody anticipated: pity.
A terraforming mission to Venus is declared a complete success. Then the survey team finds evidence that something was already living there.
In 2180, a historian specializes exclusively in the 2020s. Their lectures are standing room only and nobody can fully explain why.
An astronaut returns from a six-month mission to find that their identical twin has been living their life — and by most accounts, doing it better.
Horror: The new neighbors are perfectly friendly and have introduced themselves warmly. Their house, you notice, has no windows visible from the inside.
Every night at exactly 3:17 AM, your phone receives a text message from your own number. Tonight it says only: 'Don't go to sleep.'
A grief counselor three weeks into sessions with their most emotionally present client begins to suspect — very quietly — that the client died before the first appointment.
The town's beloved old lighthouse was automated decades ago. Someone has been trimming the wick.
A child's imaginary friend appears in the background of a family photograph taken four years before the child was born.
You inherit a farmhouse from a relative you never knew. They left behind a single index card on the kitchen counter: 'Do not count the doors.'
Participants in a sleep study begin sharing dreams in real time. The study is halted on day nine. The participants don't stop.
A hiking app logs your route even when your phone is off. The last entry was timestamped during your own funeral.
A surgeon realizes mid-operation that the patient on the table is slowly but unmistakably becoming someone else.
A pianist discovers a composition that, when played perfectly, causes every person in the audience to forget they were ever there — including themselves.
Something has been mirroring your life with perfect precision — same job, same apartment, same habits — but running exactly six months ahead of you.
An archivist cataloguing the records of a closed psychiatric facility finds the patient intake forms filled out in their own handwriting.
Your elderly neighbor asks you to water her plants while she visits family. The plants are shaped, you realize, like specific people you recognize.
A lighthouse keeper begins receiving distress signals on the emergency radio from a ship that sank in the North Atlantic in 1912.
The house you grew up in was demolished three years ago. This morning you received a piece of mail from it.
Romance: Two rival restaurant critics, both writing anonymously for competing publications, are accidentally assigned to review the same tiny, nearly impossible to find restaurant on the same evening.

A pen pal correspondence conducted entirely by handwritten letters over ten years is about to become a first face-to-face meeting. One of them has been lying about something small that now feels enormous.
A florist and a funeral director share a building wall and have argued constantly through the air vent for two years. One morning the arguments stop, and the silence is somehow worse.
Two strangers are assigned the last two seats together on a transatlantic overnight flight. One is running away from something. The other is running toward it.
A secondhand bookshop owner keeps finding annotated copies of their all-time favorite novel donated to the store — each time with more personal, more intimate notes written in the margins.
Two researchers working on opposite ends of the same unsolved problem have been anonymously correcting each other's published papers for years. They are about to meet at a conference.
A professional bridesmaid hired for her twenty-fourth wedding recognizes the officiant from somewhere she cannot immediately place.
An online chess correspondence that began as a tournament qualifier has been running for three years. Both players have run out of moves they haven't already considered together.
A restaurateur and their most loyal regular have been having nearly the same conversation every Tuesday night for five years. This Tuesday, the regular orders something completely different.
Two strangers shelter from the same unexpected storm in the same doorway and have one perfect, complete evening together before the weather breaks and the world resumes.
A letter written and sealed fifty years ago is delivered, through a long chain of errors, to entirely the wrong address. The person who receives it decides to write back.
A documentary photographer and their extremely reluctant subject are contracted to spend one week together for a profile piece neither of them agreed to willingly.
Two neighbors have been returning each other's escaped cats for so long that both have begun to suspect there may not be as many distinct cats as originally thought.
A linguist and a sign-language interpreter are both assigned to the same diplomatic summit. They spend the whole week communicating entirely through gesture and expression.
A baker begins leaving a different pastry on a stranger's doorstep every morning after finding their lost dog. The stranger starts leaving thank-you notes. The notes get longer.
Mystery: A private detective is hired to locate a missing person — and discovers that according to every available record, official and unofficial, that person has never existed.
Three strangers in three different cities each receive an identical sealed envelope on the same morning. Inside: a photograph of a house and a key that fits no lock they can find.
A forensic accountant following a decades-old financial trail across shell companies and dissolved partnerships finds that it ends with their own birth certificate.
Someone has been leaving written confessions in a church donation box every Sunday for years. No crime matching any description has ever been reported to any authority.
An art authenticator is called in to verify a newly surfaced painting. She recognizes the scene depicted — it is a place she has never been to, but has dreamed about every week for a year.
A cold case detective retires to a small coastal town expecting quiet. Their closest neighbor seems to know details about several of those cold cases that were never made public.
Every book in the victim's apartment has a single word underlined in pencil. Read in order of publication date, they form a complete sentence addressed to the detective.
A locksmith is called to open a safe sealed for forty years. Inside, among old documents, is a photograph of a person who appears to be the locksmith's parent at a young age.
Two identical crime scenes, separated by thirty years. The evidence is perfectly preserved in both — but in the older scene, it appears to have been placed there recently.
An obituary is published for someone who is very much alive, moderately furious, and now deeply interested in finding out who filed it and why.
A librarian notices that one particular book is checked out and returned on the same date every year — by a different borrower each time, none of whom remember checking it out.
A journalist investigating corruption in a small-town administration realizes that every source they interview, across days and different locations, gives them the exact same statement word for word.
A jeweler asked to resize an antique ring finds an inscription inside the band that matches a detail from an unsolved disappearance case from thirty years ago.
A historic building is demolished to clear land for development. During excavation, workers find a fully furnished room that does not appear on any blueprint or floor plan.
A translator realizes that the highly confidential document they have been paid to keep secret and render into English is a detailed confession to something that has not happened yet.
Thriller: A cybersecurity analyst brought in to contain an active ransomware attack slowly realizes, with growing dread, that the code's architecture and signature match their own work from six months ago.
A diplomatic interpreter begins deliberately mistranslating during a critical summit. They have approximately one session left to correct it before something becomes irreversible.
A whistleblower already in hiding discovers that the journalist they trusted with their evidence has a documented prior connection to the organization they are exposing.
A surgeon is informed mid-operation, via a note passed by a scrub nurse, that the patient is wired to an explosive device and someone in the surgical theater is watching via the overhead camera.
An undercover operative's cover identity is flawless — too flawless. Someone has been carefully constructing it for years before the operative was ever assigned to the case.
A crisis hostage negotiator takes a call on a case that feels eerily familiar. The voice on the other end of the line is their own.
A passenger ferry with 200 people aboard loses all communication. The coast guard's system shows the vessel arrived at port safely two hours ago.
A data analyst at an international logistics company realizes that the shipping routes they have spent months optimizing are being used to move something other than the listed cargo.
A financial investigator follows a complex money trail across four continents over eighteen months — and finds that it terminates at a charitable foundation they personally established a decade ago.
A security consultant hired to stress-test a corporation's infrastructure discovers a backdoor that was installed several years before the corporation was incorporated.
A commuter train with no mechanical faults will not slow down. The driver discovers the brakes are being overridden remotely — from a signal originating inside the passenger cars.
A journalist publishes a major investigative piece on a government cover-up. Within hours, the story begins disappearing from the internet. Then the journalist begins forgetting writing it.
A crisis negotiator is called to a hostage situation and realizes the perpetrator has transcripts of every negotiation technique they have ever used — and is using them back.
A contract operative receives a new assignment. The target is a child. They begin trying to find out who hired them before they can be reassigned to someone else.
A city infrastructure planner discovers that an emergency maintenance tunnel running beneath the financial district that was sealed in 1987 has been in regular, daily use ever since.
Poetry: Write a poem that begins with the last thing you thought about before falling asleep and ends with the first thing you thought about upon waking.
Compose a poem from the perspective of an object that has changed hands many times and absorbed each owner's particular grief.
Write a poem in which the same line appears at the beginning and end, but by the end it means something entirely different.
Compose a love poem in which the word 'love' is never used, and the beloved is never directly addressed.
Write a poem that is also a map — the reader should be able to follow it somewhere, even if that somewhere is not a physical place.
Compose a poem about a specific sound you heard today that no one else seemed to notice.
Write a poem in which the line breaks do the emotional work — where you put the pause changes everything the words mean.
Compose a poem from the final message left by something ending: a season, a friendship, a species, a star.
Write a poem in seven sentences, each exactly one line long, in which each sentence contradicts the one before it.
Compose a poem in which the speaker is giving instructions for something that cannot actually be done.
Write a poem set entirely within the duration of a single breath.
Compose an elegy for something that has not yet been lost but that you already sense slipping.
Write a poem in which the landscape around you is addressed directly, as though it might answer.
Compose a poem built from borrowed language — phrases overheard today, signs you passed, fragments half-remembered — shaped into something completely yours.
Write a poem about an ordinary Tuesday in a voice that treats it with the gravity and attention of an epic.
Historical Fiction: A Roman legionnaire stationed at Hadrian's Wall receives a letter from home that arrives years too late, bearing news that changes everything he thought he was defending.
A scribe in Alexandria copies a document that someone powerful very much does not want copied. The decision to finish it is not one they made lightly.
A woman in medieval Florence is the only person in the city who knows the plague has already arrived. She has three days before it becomes undeniable.
A samurai in the last days of the Edo period watches a steam train pass for the first time and understands, precisely and immediately, what it means for everything he has ever believed.
A court painter commissioned to idealize the king cannot make herself paint the lie with any more conviction. The portrait is due at the end of the week.
Two soldiers from opposing armies have been using the same abandoned farmhouse to sleep in on alternating nights for three months. Tonight they arrive at the same time.
An archaeologist in 1922 Cairo overhears two British officials casually dividing the contents of an unregistered tomb between themselves. She has to decide what to do with what she knows.
A lighthouse keeper on the Outer Banks during the American Civil War is given contradictory orders by two different admirals on the same morning.
The last imperial concubine of the Qing Dynasty spends the evening after the emperor's abdication trying to decide what a regular life looks like.
A suffragist chained to the gates of Parliament has been there for six hours. An older woman walks past, pauses, and keeps walking. She turns around on the next block.
A navigator on Columbus's third voyage realizes they are lost. He knows it, the captain knows it, and neither of them will say so out loud.
A translator at a major peace summit discovers that both delegations are working from different versions of the same clause. The war has not technically ended.
Adventure: A courier crossing a mountain pass in the dead of winter carries a sealed letter she is forbidden to read. Halfway through the pass, she drops it, and the seal breaks.
An expedition to map the last blank section of a continent has reached the point where the last expedition turned back. They push on for one more day.
A ship's navigator has been at sea for eleven months without sighting land. This morning the stars are wrong.
Two rival treasure hunters are the only survivors of a collapse deep underground. There is one way out and only one of them knows it.
A wilderness guide agrees to take one final expedition into territory she vowed never to return to. Her reason for going back is the same as her reason for leaving.
A city-raised journalist is assigned to spend a month embedded with a search-and-rescue team in the backcountry. On day three, they become the thing being searched for.
A climber one hundred feet from a summit decides to turn back for the first time in their career. Something about the conditions is not right, and they trust it.
A smuggler's ship is boarded by two different navies at the same time. The cargo is not what either navy was looking for, but it is worse.
A cartographer hired to complete a survey of an uncharted river begins finding marks she cannot have made herself — but that are definitely in her handwriting.
A pilot flying a solo circumnavigation lands in a country that doesn't appear on any chart she has. The runway is in good repair.
An amateur diver in the Aegean finds what might be an ancient shipwreck. The government says it doesn't exist. So does the dive shop. So does their map.
A solo sailor rounding Cape Horn in winter realizes their emergency radio has been transmitting perfectly — but no one has been responding for six days.
Flash Fiction: Write a complete story in exactly 100 words. Use the last sentence to recontextualize everything that came before it.
Two characters. One setting. One overheard sentence that changes the meaning of the preceding six months. 250 words maximum.
Tell a love story from beginning to end using only the timestamps and character names from a text message chain. No message content.
Write a ghost story in which the ghost is never mentioned — only suggested through the reactions of everyone else in the scene.
Write the first and last paragraph of a novel that does not exist. Make the reader wish it did.
A story told entirely through a series of increasingly strange items on a grocery list.
Six sentences. Two characters. The only thing they are doing is passing each other in a hallway. Make it devastating.
A story in which the important thing happens entirely in the white space between the two halves.
Write a scene in which nothing literally happens, but everything changes.
Three postcards from the same person over the course of thirty years. Same closing line each time. It means something different every time.
Tell a complete story using only dialogue. No action, no description. Every word spoken. The situation must be clear by the end.
A story in which the last sentence is the title, and the title is the last sentence. Everything in between makes it land differently each time.
Fairy Tale: A witch who has collected years from everyone she has ever wronged finds herself in possession of a life far longer than she knows what to do with.
In a kingdom where names hold power, a child is born whose name cannot be spoken without crying. The court philosophers argue about this for years.
A tailor sews three coats for three siblings: one for warmth, one for invisibility, and one for recognition by everyone who has forgotten you. Write the story of the third coat.
The dragon guarding the tower is not a captor — it is the last defense against something far worse. The hero arriving to slay it has no idea.
Every mirror in the kingdom shows not your reflection but the truest version of yourself. Most people have stopped looking.
A miller's fourth child has no extraordinary gift and no magical helper. They nonetheless achieve something the stories never account for.
A forest grants one wish to any traveler who reaches its center. Half the pilgrims turn back when they realize they don't know what they want.
An enchanted clock has been stopped at midnight for a hundred years. No one remembers why. Everyone has simply built their lives around it.
A kingdom where the royal family can only speak truth finds the youngest child born capable of a single, carefully worded lie. Everyone is afraid of what this means.
A traveling merchant sells something different to every customer — not goods, but exactly what each person was one moment away from doing without.
The chosen hero has already failed. Write the story of the completely ordinary person who now has to fix everything without any prophecy guiding them.
A cursed prince has been waiting in his tower for so long that he no longer remembers what he is waiting for — and has begun to wonder if he wants to be found.
Dystopian: In a society that has outlawed personal memory in favor of approved collective records, a woman keeps a private journal — not as resistance, but out of loneliness.
The city assigns every resident a daily happiness score. Yours has been exactly average for three years, until today, when it drops for no reason anyone can identify.
A librarian in a world where all fiction has been reclassified as misinformation hides a single novel behind the wall. She has never read it. She keeps it for someone after her.
An archivist is assigned to delete all records of a town that officially no longer exists. One file is corrupted and refuses to erase.
In a future where protest is legal but memory of it is removed within twenty-four hours, an organizer wakes on the morning after the largest march in history with no recollection of it.
A government job placement system assigns everyone their career at age sixteen based on optimization data. The protagonist has been assigned to something they are perfect for and do not want.
The last unmonitored room in the city is inside a decommissioned subway station. People come there just to be unobserved for twenty minutes. Tonight something is different.
A compliance officer enforcing a speech regulation begins to realize it is designed not to control what people say, but what they think to say.
In a world where aging has been made optional, a woman who chose to age is the oldest person in the city. She has things to say about time that no one else can hear.
A mandatory empathy implant has eliminated violent crime entirely. A psychologist is the first person in a decade to report feeling something the implant cannot categorize.
Every citizen is required to submit a weekly summary of their thoughts. One man has been submitting the same summary for eleven years. No one has noticed.
Two people meet at the border of a restricted zone. Neither is supposed to be there. Both have the same reason for crossing.
Humor: A wizard takes a corporate job at a mid-sized insurance firm. The orientation goes about as well as you would expect.
A ghost haunting a family home realizes the family is deeply boring and begins to wonder whether it is allowed to request a transfer.
A dragon retires from terrorizing the countryside and opens a bakery. The reviews are complicated by the fire hazard.
An alien anthropologist studying human behavior is deeply confused by approximately everything but is particularly troubled by queues.
A time traveler arrives in the present from the far future expecting to be amazed and is instead profoundly disappointed by almost everything.
The devil arrives to collect a soul and finds the paperwork is wrong. The soul in question is very much alive, extremely annoyed, and has questions.
A necromancer accidentally raises the dead at a family reunion. The living relatives handle this remarkably calmly. The dead are the problem.
A superhero with genuinely useful but deeply unimpressive powers tries to find a niche in a world already full of flashier options.
A talking cat is given the opportunity to speak and uses it exclusively to lodge formal complaints.
A professional villain discovers they have been accidentally enrolled in a hero loyalty program and keep accumulating points.
Death has been given a performance review. The feedback is detailed, thorough, and almost entirely about communication style.
A sentient office building develops opinions about its tenants. It starts leaving notes.
Magical Realism: In a small coastal town, fish that have been caught and returned begin speaking to the fisherman who released them. They have very little interesting to say but they are persistent.
A woman's grief for her mother begins to fill their shared house with water — not flood-level, just enough to require rubber boots and careful navigation.
Every lie told in a particular village remains visible in the air for exactly one hour. The local consequences are mostly administrative rather than moral.
A photographer discovers that her camera captures not what is there, but what the subject most deeply believes to be true. Portrait commissions have become complicated.
In a city where the dead continue their routines for one year before passing on, traffic has grown noticeably worse.
A man's lost years — the ones he spent working rather than living — return one afternoon in the form of a briefcase left on his doorstep. Inside: everything he missed.
On the day a small town's last original resident dies, the town begins forgetting itself — street names, the location of the well, the reason the fountain was built.
The rain in this story is not symbolic. It is, however, specifically targeted. The characters have begun to notice.
A librarian discovers that certain books, checked out by the right people at the right moment, will not come back until they have been fully understood.
A woman who was raised without music hears a song for the first time at forty. She doesn't understand why everyone around her can continue functioning normally after this.
A mapmaker's most recently drawn streets begin appearing in the city the morning after he finishes them. He has not yet learned to be careful what he maps.
The bees in this valley have always known something. This year, for the first time, they begin to act on it.
Memoir: Write about a place you returned to after a long absence and found different in ways you cannot precisely describe.
A single conversation with a parent or elder that you understood differently years later than you did when it happened.
Write about a skill you learned from someone who is now gone — the way they showed you, what you still hear when you use it.
Describe the house or apartment where you became an adult. Give it more credit than it deserves.
A decision you made for reasons that made sense then and make no sense now.
Write about someone who briefly occupied your life and then vanished completely — not dramatically, just gradually and then entirely.
A moment you thought would be forgettable that you have never actually forgotten.
Write about an object that outlasted the relationship it came from.
Describe what your daily routine looked like at the happiest you have been, and at the worst. Do not explain either.
The apology you never gave, or the one you gave wrong.
Write about a version of yourself from ten years ago. Try to be fair to them.
A thing you believed completely, then stopped believing, and cannot remember the exact moment you changed.

People: Draw a street performer mid-act. Use only gestural lines — capture movement and expression over accuracy.
Sketch an elderly person's hands holding an object they have owned for decades. No faces, no bodies — just the hands and what they hold.
Portray two strangers sharing an umbrella, neither of them quite comfortable with the proximity. Let the body language carry everything.
Sketch a self-portrait without looking at any reference — draw only from your imperfect memory of your own face.
Draw a musician completely lost in concentration, the background reduced to the absolute minimum of lines.
Capture the precise moment just before someone laughs — the tension in the face before the release.
Sketch a child asleep in an awkward, impossible-seeming position. Emphasize the total physical abandon of deep sleep.
Draw a figure seen only from behind, looking at something just outside the right edge of the frame.
Landscapes: Sketch a city skyline at golden hour using hatching for all shadows. Let the bare white paper stand in for every light surface.
Draw an empty park bench with only enough detail to clearly suggest the season and the weather.
Capture a narrow urban alleyway in as few deliberate lines as possible. Focus entirely on perspective, not surface detail.
Sketch a view through a window: the interior edge sharp and precisely defined, everything outside soft, loose, and impressionistic.
Draw a coastline from memory, working quickly. Let the inaccuracies and guesses become part of the composition.
Sketch a forest path that leads clearly toward something just outside the frame — something the viewer has to imagine.
Capture the exact visual edge where two weather systems meet: the clear light on one side, the approaching storm on the other.
Sketch a building in the process of being reclaimed — vines over brick, cracked concrete, nature returning.
Both: A lone figure stands at the edge of a cliff above a churning sea. Give the figure three times as many lines as you give the ocean.
Draw two people in conversation against an architectural background. Use high detail for the figures and bare suggestion for the buildings.
A child runs across an open field toward a treeline with arms spread wide. Let the landscape dwarf them completely.
A market scene with one foregrounded vendor in sharp detail and all background figures reduced to the loosest suggestion.
A silhouette stands in an open doorway; the landscape visible through it is vivid and specific; everything else is near-empty.
A traveler rests on a mountain trail midway — the path continues both behind and ahead, equally unresolved.
A figure stands at a harbor with boats softly sketched in the background. Express waiting entirely through posture.
A solitary herder with animals on a flat plain at dusk — figure, animals, horizon, and last light.
Animals: Sketch a sleeping animal from direct observation. Focus entirely on the weight and surrender of its posture, not anatomical accuracy.
Draw a bird in three stages of a single movement: before, during, and after. Use the same loose gesture lines for all three.
Sketch an insect in extreme close-up. Choose one detail — a wing, a leg joint, a compound eye — and give it the full page.
Draw a working animal performing its function. Let the purpose show in every line.
Sketch the tracks and traces an animal left behind rather than the animal itself.
Draw two animals of vastly different scale sharing the same small frame. Let the size relationship be the entire composition.
Sketch an animal mid-leap or mid-flight. Sacrifice all detail in service of the single impression of movement.
Draw an animal's face using only the minimum number of lines required for recognition. Then try to reduce it further.
Abstract: Make twenty small gestural marks in twenty seconds each. Choose three that feel most alive and develop each into a larger composition.
Draw a full-page composition using only circles and straight lines. Find everything you need within those two constraints.
Sketch your emotional state right now using only abstract line — no representational content permitted.
Create a composition based entirely on rhythmic repetition of a single mark. Let variation happen naturally, without forcing it.
Draw a response to a piece of music you are listening to right now. Match the tempo of the music with the tempo of your mark-making.
Make a large gestural composition using your non-dominant hand exclusively.
Fill the page with density and then find the composition by erasing — create through removal rather than addition.
Create an abstract composition that has a clear visual center of gravity without depicting any object. Balance is your only subject.

People: Paint a full portrait using exclusively warm tones — no cool colors anywhere in the composition.
Capture the way a single light source from one window moves across the same face at three different times of day.
Paint a crowded café scene and let the figures dissolve into each other at their edges, foreground sharp and background pure wash.
Create a portrait that begins with tight detail at the center and gradually dissolves into loose, unpigmented washes at every edge.
Paint two people mid-difficult-conversation. Express the emotional temperature entirely through warm versus cool color choices, not through expression.
A dancer in full motion — let deliberate watercolor drips run downward to suggest speed and movement.
Paint a face emerging from darkness using only wet-on-wet technique. Control nothing about the edges.
Three small portraits of the same person: content, uncertain, and overwhelmed. Same palette, different temperature balance.
Landscapes: Paint a rain-slicked street at night focusing entirely on reflections and the way color bleeds and blooms on wet surfaces.
Capture a mountain range at dusk using only five colors applied in wet-on-wet layers. Let them mix where they meet.
Paint a forest floor after heavy rainfall — moss, leaves, soft diffused light filtering down through a high canopy.
A seascape built from nothing but horizontal strokes in blues and grays of varying value. No land, no boats, no sky line.
Paint a field of summer wildflowers using primarily negative space — let the white paper serve as every bloom.
Capture the moment a storm finally breaks over flat agricultural land — a dramatic sky above a deliberately humble earth.
Paint a river at midday: the blinding bright surface reflections and the cool, deep shadows beneath the banks.
A snow-covered winter landscape using only the very palest, most diluted washes — suggest depth without reaching for dark values.
Both: Paint a lone figure standing in heavy rain while the street around them completely dissolves into abstract color washes.
A small wooden boat at sea — treat the water and the sky with equal attention and detail as you give the vessel and its occupant.
Paint a person reading in a walled garden in afternoon light. Give the surrounding foliage as much presence as the figure.
A child running through deep autumn leaves — let the warmth of the color scheme unify figure and setting completely.
A figure walking away from the viewer into thick morning fog, the landscape gradually and completely consuming them.
Two people on a clifftop: the intimate, human-scale detail of their presence set against an epic horizontal sweep of sea and open sky.
A gardener at work in their plot — let the figure and landscape share exactly the same palette so they seem to grow from each other.
A market stall at early morning: seller, produce, and pale early light all painted with equal care and equal looseness.
Animals: Paint a bird's plumage using wet-on-wet technique. Let color bleed and bloom the way feathers catch light — beyond your control.
Create a loose study of a fish underwater, letting the water and the creature share the same dissolving color palette.
Paint an animal portrait using only warm tones — no cool colors — and see what that restriction does to the sense of life in the creature.
Capture an animal in a single gestural wash. One shape, one color, one clear impression — completed before the wash dries.
Paint a scene of animals at water — drinking, wading, reflected — and give the reflection equal attention to what it mirrors.
Create three small studies of the same animal in three different lights: early morning, high noon, and dusk.
Paint an insect on a plant or flower. Give both subject and support equal color attention and equal care.
Paint an animal you have seen recently, from memory rather than reference. Let the inaccuracies tell their own story.
Abstract: Lay down large, wet washes of color without any plan. When the surface is nearly dry, begin responding to the shapes that have already formed.
Create an abstract composition based entirely on the tension between one warm and one cool color mixed at different ratios across the page.
Pour or drop paint onto wet paper and tilt the surface to direct the flow. Your job is to decide when to stop, not where the paint goes.
Create a composition using only the residue of dried washes — the tidelines, the blooms, the granulation. The accidents are the painting.
Paint a series of overlapping transparent layers, each slightly different in hue, and observe what happens to color where they intersect.
Create an abstract response to a single remembered emotion — not an illustration of it, but a direct translation into color and mark.
Work wet-on-wet across a very large surface with no sketch and no plan. The painting is a conversation, not a statement.
Create a controlled abstract in which every mark was deliberately placed — the opposite of accident. Control is the subject.

People: Create a portrait using a hard restriction: exactly four colors, flat fills, and hard geometric edges. No gradients.
Design a character from a world where emotional states are expressed through visible bioluminescence beneath the skin.
Illustrate a person's true personality as their cast shadow — let the shadow tell the story the person themselves would never show.
Create a character portrait in the compositional and color language of a vintage travel poster from the 1930s or 1950s.
Design a figure built entirely from the objects that define their identity — profession, obsessions, memories — with no traditional anatomy visible.
Illustrate the same person twice in one composition: the version they present to the world, and the version that exists at 3am.
Create a portrait using only geometric shapes. Explore how few shapes you need before recognition completely breaks down.
Design a character set 500 years in the future. Let their clothing, tools, and modifications communicate their entire biography.
Landscapes: Create an environment that exists somewhere between a specific dream you remember and a specific memory you're not certain is real.
Design a complete biome that does not exist on Earth. Make it feel ecologically plausible — there should be a logic to what lives there.
Illustrate the same landscape at dawn, midday, dusk, and deep night in four equal panels. Same composition, different light.
Create a cityscape from an impossible point of view — from underground looking up, from orbit looking down, or from inside a building's wall.
Design a planet landscape where the light source is a different color than our sun. Let every shadow and every highlight reflect that fact.
Illustrate a place that was once a major city, now fully reclaimed by vegetation and wildlife, set 200 years from now.
Create a detailed environment for a world with inverted gravity — where water flows upward and things fall toward the sky.
Design a landscape where the weather itself is a living entity visibly moving through and interacting with the environment.
Both: Illustrate a person standing at the center of an environment that directly mirrors their current emotional state — the landscape is their interiority made visible.
Create a scene in which a figure's physical presence is visibly and slowly altering the landscape around them with every step.
Design a book cover illustration for a novel that does not exist but that you wish did. Title and author of your own invention.
Illustrate two figures at vastly different scales in the same landscape. Explore what changes about the story depending on which one you look at.
Create an illustration depicting the final scene of an imaginary epic — make the weight of everything that came before it visible.
Design an editorial illustration about the relationship between human beings and the technology that now mediates most of their experience.
Illustrate a myth or legend from a culture other than your own. Spend time researching the story and its visual traditions before you begin.
Create a double-exposure composition: one figure and one landscape that, overlaid, tell a single coherent story.
Animals: Design a creature that could plausibly exist — an evolutionary adaptation to a specific real environment no existing species has fully exploited.
Create a character design for an anthropomorphized animal that maintains its animal nature completely even while being humanized in dress or posture.
Illustrate an animal portrait in a flat, graphic style — no gradients, limited palette, strong silhouette. Let the design breathe.
Create a scientific illustration of an imaginary species: a classification plate with multiple views, a scale indicator, and detailed feature callouts.
Design a pattern or repeat based entirely on a single animal type — abstracted to whatever degree the design requires.
Illustrate an animal in its habitat at a moment of behavioral interest — hunting, displaying, nesting, or resting.
Create a series of three spot illustrations of the same animal at three different life stages.
Design an animal as it might be rendered in the visual language of a specific cultural tradition — Japanese woodblock, Mughal miniature, or medieval bestiary.
Abstract: Create a generative-feeling composition: design a simple rule for placing marks and follow it rigidly until the image is complete.
Design a composition using only two colors — one hue and one neutral — and explore how far you can push contrast and variety within those constraints.
Create an abstract composition where the focal point is achieved entirely through contrast of scale, not color or value.
Design a pattern that reads as both random and structured depending on how closely you look at it.
Create an abstract work inspired by a specific sound. Translate frequency, texture, and duration into visual equivalents.
Design a composition using only geometric primitives that nonetheless evokes a clear emotional response through proportion and placement.
Create an abstract work in which the negative space carries as much visual weight as the positive forms.
Design a series of four abstract compositions that form a sequence — a visual sentence with a beginning, middle, complication, and resolution.

People: Paint a figure using Rembrandt lighting — one side fully illuminated, the other almost completely lost in rich, warm shadow.
Create a portrait of a person in their workspace, surrounded by the specific tools and evidence of their craft.
Paint a range of skin tones using only earth pigments — raw sienna, burnt umber, yellow ochre, and titanium white. No shortcuts.
Create a figure study in which the drapery is as expressive and as carefully observed as the face.
Paint two people in a domestic interior. Let the physical space between them carry all of the emotional weight of the scene.
Create a portrait using a heavy impasto technique. Let the texture and the marks of the palette knife express something that smooth blending cannot.
Paint a figure in motion using a restricted palette of no more than three hues plus white. Color temperature does the work of form.
Create a large-scale portrait of someone you know well, from direct observation if at all possible.
Landscapes: Paint a landscape in the tradition of the Hudson River School — monumental scale, dramatic raking light, humanity made small.
Go outside and make a plein air study on location. Accept completely the challenges of changing light and imperfect conditions.
Paint a storm at sea as the Romantic painters understood it — nature as an overwhelming, indifferent, sublime force.
Create a moonlit night landscape using cold blue-violet shadow tones and the warmest possible light reserved for the moon itself.
Paint a winter landscape with primary attention to the subtle color variation within snow — the warm shadows, the cool highlights.
Create a landscape in which the sky occupies no less than three quarters of the total canvas area.
Paint a garden in peak midsummer — the controlled, pressurized wildness of everything growing at full capacity.
Paint the same view three times in three different amounts of detail: distant, middle, and close. Study what changes.
Both: Paint a figure in a landscape where the palette of their clothing deliberately, purposefully clashes with the surrounding environment.
Create a pastoral scene — a person completely at rest in nature — and treat stillness itself as your primary compositional challenge.
Paint a figure working outdoors in extreme or dramatic weather. Let the landscape appear completely indifferent to their effort.
Create a scene of outdoor celebration — a festival, a gathering, a moment of collective joy — exploring the relationship between happiness and open space.
Paint a single figure in a vast landscape. Determine the minimum size a figure can be before their humanity disappears entirely.
Create an allegory: a figure in a landscape where figure and setting together represent an abstract concept — grief, hope, time, or change.
Paint a large, populated outdoor scene — a market, a festival, a demonstration — where people are woven inseparably into a specific, recognizable place.
Create a large landscape with a single small figure positioned anywhere in the composition. Consider carefully how that placement changes everything.
Animals: Paint an animal portrait with the same psychological weight and compositional intention you would bring to a human portrait.
Create a large-scale predator-prey scene. Resist both sentimentality and sensationalism — paint what actually is.
Paint a domestic animal in the precise quality of indoor light: the specific warmth of lamp light or the cold northern light through a window.
Study a single bird and paint it with the level of scientific attention of Audubon. Accuracy is the art.
Paint an animal at the edge of a habitat boundary — the shoreline, the forest edge, the place where one world gives way to another.
Create an oil study of an animal's texture — feather, scale, fur, or hide — filling the canvas with pure surface and light.
Paint a nocturnal animal in low, cool light. Let the dark dominate and let your subject emerge reluctantly from it.
Create an animal painting in the tradition of Dutch Golden Age still life — a dead game bird, a caught fish — with the same reverence and technical ambition.
Abstract: Create a large abstract composition using only a palette knife — no brushes. Let texture, direction, and accumulation of paint be your entire vocabulary.
Work on a very dark ground. Build your composition from light into dark, reversing the typical painting sequence.
Create an abstract composition responding to a specific place — not depicting it, but carrying its quality of light, space, and atmosphere.
Make a painting using only primary colors and their direct mixtures. No pre-mixed colors from a tube.
Create an abstract work where every mark is committed and irreversible — no overpainting, no corrections. First marks are final.
Build a composition through layering: underpainting first, then opaque mid-tones, then transparent glazes. Let all three layers remain visible.
Create an oil painting that is almost entirely one color. Find everything you need in value, temperature, and texture.
Paint in a format radically different from what you usually choose — very tall, very wide, very small. Let the format generate the composition.

People: Create a bold portrait in a pop art sensibility — flat color areas, strong black outlines, high contrast, and primary palette.
Paint a complete figure study using only palette knives. No brushes permitted at any stage of the work.
Create a portrait painted directly over a collaged layer of newspaper, found text, or torn magazine pages.
Paint a figure study using a palette inspired entirely by a specific time of day that you find emotionally resonant.
Create a portrait using a split-complementary color scheme — choose one primary and the two colors flanking its complement.
Paint a figure that is in the process of merging into or emerging from a highly graphic, repeating patterned background.
Create a portrait with the graphic sensibility of 1970s design — flat color shapes, warm earthy palette, bold simplified forms.
Paint four small rapid portraits in one session, spending no more than twenty minutes on each. Commit to speed over refinement.
Landscapes: Create a vivid, saturated landscape in colors from the opposite of reality — a deep purple sky, a red sea, a yellow mountain.
Paint an entire landscape using only a palette knife. No brushes at any point. Texture is now your primary descriptive tool.
Create a night city scene using neon accent colors pushing against a near-black background — sign light, window light, reflections.
Build a landscape using masking tape to create hard, clean geometric divisions between sky, land, and water.
Create an abstract interpretation of a real place you know well — keep the geography, abandon the realistic color.
Paint a landscape that reads completely differently at arm's length than it does from across the room.
Create a triptych: three same-sized panels showing the identical view in three different seasons.
Paint a landscape with a deliberately graphic, poster-like quality — simplified silhouettes, strong horizon, bold flat shapes.
Both: Paint a street scene using bold, flattened graphic shapes. Simplify both people and buildings until they nearly become abstraction.
Create a mural-scale composition — even if small in actual dimension — depicting a figure or group in a place that is personally meaningful.
Paint an outdoor festival or celebration scene in vivid, high-chroma color. Embrace the chaos of many people in an open outdoor space.
Create a composition where the pattern or texture of a figure's clothing continues visually and seamlessly into the landscape behind them.
Paint a mythological or folkloric figure placed specifically and incongruously in a real, contemporary, recognizable landscape.
Create a painting that begins with precise representation at its center and becomes increasingly abstract toward every edge.
Paint a transit waiting space — a bus stop, train platform, or ferry queue — focusing on ordinary people in ordinary in-between time.
Create a bright, graphic illustration of a folk tale or childhood story using the full chromatic range of your palette.
Animals: Paint an animal using the bold, graphic language of a linocut or woodblock print — strong outlines, flat color shapes, high contrast.
Create a brightly colored animal portrait that deliberately departs from the creature's natural coloring. What does invented color do to your reading of the animal?
Paint an animal in a pop art style: flat fills, strong outline, limited palette, graphic impact.
Create a patterned background and paint an animal against it. Let the pattern either camouflage or dramatically reveal the creature.
Paint a swarm, flock, or school — many identical animals in motion — focusing on the collective shape rather than any individual creature.
Create an animal painting in pure acrylic impasto — palette knife only, heavy texture, no brushes. Surface becomes the subject.
Paint an animal in an unexpected, incongruous setting — a wild creature in an urban space, a domestic animal in wild nature.
Create a diptych: the same animal in its native habitat and in an entirely different, invented one.
Abstract: Create an abstract painting using only masking tape to generate hard-edged geometric compositions. Remove and reveal as you build.
Paint with a palette of only colors you mixed yourself — no colors used directly from the tube.
Create a large abstract in which the primary visual interest comes from surface texture rather than color or composition.
Build a composition using poured paint. Tilt, direct, and stop the flow rather than painting in the conventional sense.
Create an abstract triptych in which each panel is visually complete alone but the three together say something none can say individually.
Paint an abstract response to a memory — not an illustration of it, but a translation into color, mark, and surface.
Create a painting that is in conversation with a specific abstract work by another artist — not a copy, but a response.
Build an abstract painting from the inside out — start with the center point of visual interest and work outward to the edges.

People: Draw a complete portrait using only cross-hatching — no outlines at any point. Every edge and every form must be built entirely from intersecting lines.
Create a portrait using a single continuous line. The pen never leaves the paper from the first mark to the last.
Illustrate a figure in active motion using only ink splatter, flicks, and fast gestural marks. Deliberate accidents only.
Draw a crowded scene where every figure exists at a different dilution of ink — densest black in the foreground, palest wash in the distance.
Create five small portraits of five different faces using nothing but stippling — dots of varying density building every form.
Draw a figure using negative space as your primary tool — the ink defines everything surrounding the figure, and the figure itself remains as bare paper.
Illustrate a person's emotional state using entirely non-representational ink marks. No recognizable figure. Pure mark-making.
Create a portrait using large, committed brushstrokes in the spirit of sumi-e. No corrections. No hesitation. First marks are final marks.
Landscapes: Draw a dense forest using only vertical lines. Let the closeness of the lines control how dark each section becomes.
Create a seascape built from horizontal ink strokes of varying weight and varying spacing. Nothing else in the composition.
Draw a city skyline at night using ink wash — build the atmospheric glow from multiple layers of increasingly diluted ink.
Illustrate a mountain landscape using only stippling. Every shadow, every gradient, every surface — nothing but dots.
Draw a stormy sky using the broadest, most aggressive brushstrokes you are willing to make.
Create a landscape where the foreground is rendered in the most obsessive detail you can manage and the background is almost completely bare.
Draw a river or waterfall using only flowing, calligraphic lines. The line quality itself should feel like moving water.
Create a night scene using ink wash where the white of the paper is the absolute brightest point and undiluted black ink is used only for the single darkest accent.
Both: Draw a figure on a bridge above a city. Use sharp, precise lines for the figure and loose ink wash for everything beneath and around them.
Create an illustration in the tradition of classic pen-and-ink book illustrations: one figure in one richly detailed environment.
Draw a scene from folklore or mythology using bold, confident brushwork. No tentative marks.
Create a single comics panel — one isolated moment — and make every compositional and mark-making choice serve the story.
Draw a figure moving through a landscape that communicates unfamiliarity and strangeness — somewhere the figure clearly does not belong.
Illustrate the feeling of a specific piece of music or a specific poem: one figure in one landscape that captures the emotional register of the words or sounds.
Create a wide panoramic landscape with figures rendered as pure silhouette in the foreground, ink wash handling the entire sky.
Draw a market, bazaar, or street food scene: dense cross-hatching for the shadows under awnings, ink wash for the open-air atmosphere.
Animals: Draw an animal portrait using only cross-hatching. Every form and every shadow built entirely from crossed lines — no outline anywhere.
Create a single continuous line drawing of an animal in motion. The pen never leaves the page.
Illustrate a bird in the style of a naturalist field journal — crisp, observational, with annotated details and behavioral notes.
Draw an animal using ink wash only, no line work. Build form and surface from dilution and pooling of ink alone.
Create a close-up insect study using dense stippling for every shadow and every surface transition.
Draw an underwater animal using flowing, calligraphic brushstrokes. Let the quality of the mark suggest both creature and element.
Illustrate an animal hunt or chase in a single sequential composition — the full arc of the story in one contained ink drawing.
Create a bold, graphic animal print in the tradition of Japanese woodblock: strong silhouette, flat planes, decisive mark-making.
Abstract: Create an abstract composition using only ink wash — different dilutions, different pooling strategies, no line work.
Dip an unconventional tool into ink and make a full composition using only what that tool can produce.
Create a composition that is entirely made from the controlled bleed of ink into wet paper.
Build an abstract composition using only geometric marks — squares, lines, circles — with no organic shapes allowed anywhere.
Create a large gestural abstract using a wide brush and very diluted ink. Speed is required. Deliberation is not.
Make a composition where the white of the paper is the primary visual element. The ink defines only what is not white.
Create an abstract composition by overprinting — make a mark, let it dry, make another mark over it, repeat until the composition resolves.
Build an abstract ink composition inspired by a specific landscape — its textures, rhythms, and spatial qualities — without depicting it.

People: Create a detailed character sprite for a game that does not exist. Include at minimum an idle, a walking cycle, and one action frame.
Design a pixel portrait of a specific person using a palette restricted to exactly sixteen colors.
Create a set of pixel emotion icons using the same face — eight different emotional states from the same base character.
Design a fantasy RPG character class of your own invention. The silhouette alone should communicate the role.
Create the same portrait at three sizes — 16x16, 32x32, and 64x64 pixels. Observe how complexity and expressiveness change at each scale.
Design a villain whose visual design makes their core motivation immediately readable without any text or context.
Create a complete pixel character sheet — front view, back view, side view, and three distinct facial expressions.
Design a non-human character — a construct, a creature, a spirit — that nonetheless communicates immediately recognizable and relatable emotion.
Landscapes: Create a pixel landscape at three different times of day in three equal panels. Let only the palette change — not the composition.
Design a single-screen game environment that tells a complete story about who lives there without any characters present.
Create a pixel seascape with animated water — sketch the keyframes of a three-frame looping animation.
Design a fantasy biome in pixel art where the palette itself communicates the climate, the danger level, and the dominant weather.
Create a dungeon room using tile-based logic — design a space that would be interesting to navigate and that rewards exploration.
Design the same sky at four distinct times of day as four isolated horizontal strips. Study how palette drives atmosphere entirely.
Create a pixel cityscape — future, historical, or alternate — where the era is completely legible in the architectural details.
Design an outdoor environment from a specific real decade in history. Make the time period identifiable in every visual detail.
Both: Create a single-screen pixel scene in which one character in one environment together tell a complete wordless story.
Design a title screen for an imaginary game — character, setting, title treatment, and overall mood in a single unified composition.
Create a pixel diorama: a character placed within a home environment that reflects their personality in every furnishing and detail.
Design a single story beat as a cutscene panel — the most pivotal moment in an imaginary narrative, expressed through composition alone.
Create a pixel art recreation or reinterpretation of a famous painting, translated fully into pixel-native visual language.
Design a small overworld map icon: a distinct figure standing in or before a distinct environment type.
Create a pixel animation key frame — a figure in a landscape at a single moment of high dramatic tension.
Design an NPC character and their home environment as a single cohesive visual package — the space and the person should feel inseparable.
Animals: Create an animated pixel creature — design a four-frame idle animation that communicates the animal's personality without moving the whole body.
Design a fantasy creature for a game bestiary entry: top-down overworld sprite and front-facing battle sprite.
Create a pixel art nature scene populated by animals, where each creature's scale and placement feels ecologically plausible.
Design a series of six animal icons at 16x16 pixels — test the limits of recognition at extreme small scale.
Create a pixel art pet companion character with three emotional states: happy, anxious, and sleepy.
Design a dangerous creature for a game — its threat level should be legible in the silhouette alone without any additional context.
Create a detailed pixel art study of a single species: multiple views, color variants, and one behavioral pose.
Design an impossible hybrid creature — two species combined — and make the combination feel visually and conceptually coherent.
Abstract: Create an abstract pixel composition using only a 4-color palette. Let pattern and proportion do all the work.
Design a seamlessly tiling abstract pattern at 16x16 pixels that reads as both geometric and organic simultaneously.
Create a pixel animation of a pure abstract transition — one color field becoming another through a designed sequence of intermediate states.
Build an abstract pixel composition based entirely on the visual properties of dithering — the pattern itself becomes the image.
Design a pixel gradient that cycles through a full emotional arc — from one mood-color at the left edge to a completely different one at the right.
Create a large abstract pixel composition where the pixel grid itself is visible as part of the design intention.
Design an abstract pixel background for a game: seamlessly tiling, visually interesting at a distance, non-distracting in use.
Create a pixel composition inspired by circuit board topology — traces, nodes, and pathways — as pure abstract composition.

People: Create a portrait that combines printed or cut photographic fragments with hand-drawn or hand-painted elements. Let the seam between them be visible.
Build a figure from torn paper, found textures, and ink on a mixed surface. The construction process should be part of what the viewer sees.
Create a portrait in which the background is composed entirely of handwritten text — journal entries, letters, lists — relevant to the subject.
Combine watercolor washes for the large forms with gel pen detail work for the fine marks. Let the contrast between wet and precise be your primary texture.
Create a figure using fabric scraps, thread, and paint on a mixed substrate. The figure can be sewn as much as drawn or painted.
Build a portrait where the subject is painted or drawn and the surrounding background is constructed from maps, letters, printed ephemera, or found documents.
Combine relief printing or rubber stamp textures as an underlay with painted or drawn figures worked over the top.
Create a self-portrait using only whatever materials are physically present in your immediate space right now. Quantity and variety are not the point.
Landscapes: Create a landscape using hand-painted paper that you then cut into depth planes and layer physically over one another.
Build a landscape incorporating actual collected natural materials — pressed leaves, dried soil, bark, sand — fixed permanently to the surface.
Create a topographic landscape by layering torn and folded paper into physical raised contour ridges across the surface.
Combine a printed photograph with hand-painted or drawn additions and subtractions to create something the original photograph never was.
Build a seascape incorporating actual sand, salt crystals, or translucent layers of medium to create physical surface depth.
Create a cityscape collage assembled entirely from architectural fragments cut from magazines, maps, technical drawings, and printed documents.
Layer tissue paper, ink wash, and deliberate mark-making to create a landscape of pure atmospheric mood without literal description.
Build a landscape from entirely unexpected materials — packaging, labels, receipts, ticket stubs — and discover what world they want to become.
Both: Create a scene in which the figure is painted or drawn in a realistic mode and the landscape surrounding them is built entirely from collage.
Build a narrative image — one person in one place — where every distinct element is created from a different material or technique.
Create a response to a specific piece of music you are listening to right now. Let the figure and the landscape both be shaped by what you hear.
Combine printmaking with painting: ink a textured surface and print it as your ground layer, then paint or draw figures directly over the printed texture.
Build a portrait or landscape that is materially of a specific decade — use actual ephemera from that era as your primary building material.
Create a mythological or folkloric scene using mixed media. Choose materials that comment on or deepen the meaning of the story being depicted.
Combine embroidery or hand-stitching with paint or ink. Let thread become line, let fabric become ground, let stitch become mark.
Create an image with a hidden layer — something concealed within or beneath the visible materials that only close physical examination will reveal.
Animals: Create an animal portrait incorporating actual natural materials — feathers, pressed leaves, sand — fixed to the surface permanently.
Build an insect collage using torn paper, transparent overlays, and fine ink detailing. Layer transparency to suggest the creature's delicacy.
Create an animal portrait combining a printed photographic base with hand-painted color and drawn detail added over it.
Build an underwater scene using layers of translucent medium, tissue paper, and ink — let the layering create the sense of depth and diffusion.
Create a bird study combining watercolor for the large color shapes and pen for the precise feather detail. Let the two media remain visibly distinct.
Build a wild animal portrait using torn magazine pages for the textured background and paint or ink for the creature itself.
Create an animal in a habitat built from actual maps of that habitat's real geography, combined with painted or drawn representation of the animal.
Make an animal portrait where the creature is drawn or painted and surrounded by handwritten field observations and quick gestural studies from real or imagined fieldwork.
Abstract: Create an abstract composition using only materials that are themselves transparent or translucent — tracing paper, tissue, acetate, glass, water.
Build an abstract collage using only printed text as material. Cut, tear, overlap, and layer until the words dissolve into pure visual form.
Create an abstract work that begins as a drawing, becomes a painting, and ends as a collage — all three stages must remain visible.
Make an abstract composition from a single material used multiple different ways — paper folded, torn, painted, printed on, and stitched.
Create a textured abstract using wax resist, ink wash, and salt. Prepare the surface, apply the resist, pour the ink, add the salt. Then wait.
Build an abstract composition using exclusively found materials from today's environment — receipts, wrappers, torn corners, fabric scraps.
Create an abstract work where stitching or thread is the primary linear element, combined with a painted or dyed ground.
Make an abstract piece in two clearly distinct materials where the contrast between them is the visual and conceptual subject.

People: Build a portrait using only broad side-of-stick marks — establish form through value mass rather than contour. Let the smudge be a tool, not an accident.
Draw a figure seated by a window. Restrict yourself to three distinct values: white paper, mid-grey, and deep black. No blending between them.
Capture a face using exclusively erasing as your drawing method. Begin with full charcoal coverage and pull the light forward.
Build a portrait from charcoal dust, your palm, and a single sharp tool. No deliberate outlines — construct entirely through value.
Sketch a standing figure in a single continuous gesture using a large stick and full arm movement. No wrist drawing, no lifting.
Draw a figure mid-gesture with arms extended. Prioritize movement over accuracy. Commit to every mark.
A face emerging from or receding into complete darkness. Give the shadow equal compositional weight to the features themselves.
A crowd reduced to value masses — no individuals, just the collective weight of people together in light and dark.
Landscapes: Cover the full page in charcoal, then use an eraser to pull the landscape from the darkness. Light exists only where you remove material.
Draw the silhouette of a tree line at dusk using only broad marks. Let the edge of the charcoal mass carry the entire mood.
A winter landscape in pure black, white, and three greys. Use value contrast alone to define space without any other tool.
Draw rolling farmland using only horizontal marks of varying weight. Let the density of marks create the distinction between near and far.
A stormy sky taking up three-quarters of the page. Give the land only what the sky cannot contain.
Draw a rocky cliff face using only marks that follow the direction of the geological formation. Let structural logic drive every mark.
A city at night using smudge and fog to soften edges between buildings and sky. Hard edges only for the closest foreground plane.
Draw an empty road disappearing into a dense forest. Use stark contrast — pale light on the road, deep shadow under the trees.
Both: A figure standing in bright snow. Use the extreme value contrast of charcoal and white paper without mid-tones. Make the cold visible.
A crowd under a streetlight at night. Use the light to create the focal point and let everyone at the edges dissolve into near-black.
Draw a figure walking into a landscape using the same grey value for both. Only their edge against each other defines the separation.
A solitary figure on a wide, flat beach at low tide. Allocate the ground so the person is small and the space enormous.
Two people by a fire in an open field at night. Use the fire as the only light source; let it actively fight the surrounding darkness.
A woman hanging laundry on a rooftop above a city. Make the domestic detail precise and let the city below fall into impression.
A fisherman on a dock at first light. Let the tonal mass of fog unify the figure and the water.
A lone figure in a winter wood surrounded by bare trees. Use the vertical rhythm of the trunks to create depth and enclosure.
Animals: Sketch a large animal in motion using no more than five marks. Make each mark count for form, weight, and direction.
Draw a sleeping dog in full charcoal, focusing entirely on the warmth and weight of its resting body — muscle and fur, not outline.
Use only the smudge tool to build a portrait of a cat's face. No hard marks. Every edge arrived at through gradual transition.
A bird of prey mid-flight — stretch the wingspan edge to edge. Work fast and commit to the gesture.
Draw a herd of animals in a landscape so that individual creatures read as shapes within a larger collective mass.
An animal at close range looking directly out of the picture plane. Use the intensity of charcoal black to create depth in the eyes.
A horse in profile using only five values: coat, mane, shadow, highlight, and background — each a distinct flat plane.
Sketch an insect in extreme close-up. Use a sharp charcoal point for precise carapace detail and broader marks for the surrounding dark.
Abstract: Fill the page with charcoal completely. Work back with erasers of three different hardnesses to find a composition that was already there.
Make ten marks in ten different ways using the same piece of charcoal — edge, tip, flat, smudge, scratch. See what conversation emerges.
Build a composition from nothing but value gradients. No edges. No forms. Just the movement from dark to light and back again.
Create a rhythm across the page using a single repeated mark in three values. Let the repetition become the subject.
Draw an emotional state using exclusively charcoal marks. Resist any figurative interpretation. Work until it feels accurate.
A composition based entirely on the contrast between something precise and something dissolved. Define neither. Let the relationship speak.
Make marks that move in all directions from a single point of maximum darkness. Let the energy radiate and slow at the edges.
Create a large composition using only two tools: a wide charcoal block and a kneaded eraser. Mark and removal are your only methods.

People: Paint a portrait using completely flat color shapes — no gradients, no blending, no transitions. Use edge and shape alone to convey form.
Capture a figure in interior light using a muted palette of five colors maximum. Resist the impulse to render.
A face using three values per color: shadow tone, mid tone, and light. Lay each shape cleanly and do not soften the edges.
Build a figure using only opaque, flat strokes placed side by side. Think in shapes, not lines.
Paint a portrait that begins with the background color and builds the figure entirely in front of it using opaque layers.
A figure in outdoor light — use the opacity of gouache to correct and refine. Show the reworking; do not hide it.
Two figures in silhouette against a bright ground. Give the silhouettes complex, accurate shapes but fill them with flat color.
Paint someone reading using only the shapes of shadow and highlight. The lit and unlit planes should tell the whole story.
Landscapes: A flat, graphic rendering of a cityscape using only five colors. Use shape and value contrast to create depth without tonal blending.
Paint a landscape at a specific hour of the day using the exact quality of light as your primary compositional challenge.
A botanical close-up — a single leaf, branch, or flower in flat gouache shapes. Work from direct observation.
Paint the same landscape in two different color moods: warm and cool. Work fast. Both versions on the same page.
A graphic rendering of a body of water using flat horizontal stripes of color in carefully graduated values.
A mountain seen from distance rendered as flat shapes of color. No gradients in the sky; no blending in the mountain.
Paint a garden using opaque color blocking. Give equal visual weight to the empty spaces between plants.
A coastal scene using no more than four colors. Use value within those four to supply the full range you need.
Both: A figure in a graphic landscape. Build the scene in planes — foreground, middle, background — treating each separately.
Paint a market scene using flat simplified shapes. The people become repeating color forms against the architecture.
A boat at anchor in a harbor. Make the water flat stripes; make the boat and its reflection precise graphic shapes.
Two children in a summer garden — make the color choices warm, high-key, and fully opaque.
A street scene at dusk — give the lamplight a specific color temperature and let everything around it respond accordingly.
A figure at an open window. Use the window to create a strong compositional division between interior and exterior light.
Farmland with a single worker in the distance. Use scale and color saturation to establish near and far.
A person on a park bench in autumn. Match the person and the foliage in color temperature so they belong to the same world.
Animals: Paint a bird using flat, clean gouache shapes. Reference the approach of Japanese woodblock artists toward simplified form.
A dog portrait using opaque, flat color areas arranged to describe the face. Use edge quality, not blending, to distinguish fur from background.
An insect or small creature in extreme close-up, painted flat and precise. Treat it as a graphic design problem.
A fish underwater using flat planes of color for water, light, and scale pattern.
Two birds on a wire. Simple, balanced, graphic. Give the negative space as much consideration as the birds.
Paint a wild animal using bold, simplified color blocking. Reduce the form to the minimum number of shapes required.
A cat stretched in a sunny patch — use the flat graphic shadow shape as much as the lit form itself.
An animal at rest seen from above — use the unusual angle to flatten the form and simplify it further.
Abstract: Using only six pre-mixed colors, build a composition by layering opaque shapes — each one reacting to the shape before it.
Create a composition that works like graphic design — flat shapes, bold contrast, precise edges throughout.
Mix a limited palette of three colors plus white. See how far the palette extends when pushed with careful layering.
Build a composition by beginning with a dark background tone and working from dark to light using opaque marks.
A purely geometric composition — only rectangles and circles — but with a clear emotional or atmospheric intent.
Mix a palette of low-saturation, complex tones and build a full composition using only those tones.
Explore what happens when you leave the underpainting visible in some areas and fully cover it in others.
