Create a composition based on a specific piece of architecture, rendered entirely through shape, color, and arrangement — no rendering.

People: Build a portrait using only soft pastel dust applied with a finger. No marks — only smearing and layering of color.
A figure in strong sidelight — use warm pastels in the light and cool in the shadow. Let the contrast carry the form.
Capture a person dancing using bold gestural pastel strokes that blur and overlap. Accept the imprecision as intention.
A seated figure using the texture of the paper as a deliberate element — let the tooth show through in lighter passages.
Build a face using only the side of the pastel stick. No sharp marks, only broad color forms.
Two people sharing a moment. Use warm, close colors for both to make them feel connected through the palette alone.
A child's face — use the softest, most atmospheric approach you can. Layered, gentle, slightly out of focus.
A portrait that begins with a coloured ground — build the face by adding and subtracting from a middle value base.
Landscapes: A sunset sky using layered pastels blended with your fingers. Work quickly, from light to dark, before the colors muddy.
A misty morning landscape built from tonal gradients of smeared horizontal strokes — soft, directionless, atmospheric.
A field of flowers in full bloom — use saturated pastel color and let the paper texture contribute the feeling of light on petals.
A coastal scene at dawn. Use cool blues and warm golds directly against each other with minimal blending.
A snow-covered landscape using only pale lavender, grey-blue, and yellow-pink marks against white paper.
A forest interior — use layering to suggest dappled light without rendering any individual leaf or branch.
A dramatic sky above flat marshland. Give three-quarters of the composition to the sky and make every inch of it work.
A mountain at golden hour. Let the layering of warm over cool tones create the specific quality of alpine light.
Both: A figure in a wildflower meadow — let the pastel color blur the boundary between person and landscape.
A person by a large window with a landscape behind them — use warm interior versus cool exterior to separate the two spaces.
Two people on a hillside with a wide landscape below — use aerial perspective, cool and pale in the distance, warm and saturated near.
A silhouette of a figure at the edge of a cliff at sunset. Make the sky the real subject and the figure a note in it.
A child running through autumn leaves. Match the warm earth tones of the leaves to the warmth of the figure.
A figure standing in fog. Use the softest available pastel and the lightest possible touch throughout.
A market scene rendered in impressionistic pastel strokes — movement, color, and light over precise observation.
A person sitting at the edge of water with reflections. Let the landscape double and soften beneath the still surface.
Animals: Draw a sleeping cat using soft, warm pastels that blur into the surface beneath them.
A horse in a field — use the broad strokes of the pastel stick to suggest the sweep of the animal's body.
A close-up of feathers — use layered color and directional marks to suggest texture without rendering each quill.
A dog's face using warm, heavy pastel marks in the light and cool thin marks in the shadow.
An animal in motion — let the blurring and smearing of pastel become the suggestion of speed itself.
Two birds on a branch — use the negative space of the paper against saturated marks to create the impression of surrounding light.
A large animal filling the composition. Use the scale of the subject to push the pastel into bold, broad territory.
A fish underwater — use cool blues and greens blended together with occasional bright marks for scale and light.
Abstract: Apply five bold pastel colors in large horizontal bands. Then smudge each into the next until the bands become a gradient landscape.
Begin with a fully saturated pastel ground. Work into it by adding lighter marks and see what composition emerges from the color.
Create a composition from purely gestural marks — fast, expressive, directional. Accept whatever the pastel decides.
Use complementary colors in direct contact. Blend them at the edges; leave them raw and hard at the center.
Build a composition entirely through subtraction — begin with a fully covered page and reveal the composition through erasure.
Make a composition that is about atmospheric depth: near dark, far light, everything in between graduated.
Apply color in layers — each layer a slightly different temperature. Blend selectively. Let the underlayers breathe through.
Use only the full stick of pastel, laid flat, to make all marks. See how much variation this single position offers.

People: A portrait built up through careful cross-hatching in four or five layered colors. Work slowly; the medium rewards patience.
Draw a figure in bright outdoor light using burnishing to achieve richness in the highlighted areas.
A close-up of a face focused on the texture and color variation of skin — not tone alone, but the underlying warmth and coolness.
A child asleep — work with the lightest possible touch and let the delicacy of the medium carry the tenderness.
A face in profile with sharp sidelight — use the white of the paper for full lights and build value slowly from there.
A full figure in interior light. Use cross-hatching at multiple angles to build optical color mixing in the shadow areas.
Draw hands in careful detail — focus on the complex texture and color shifts across knuckles, palms, and fingertips.
A portrait using layered wax bloom as a deliberate texture element. Let the surface quality become part of the image.
Landscapes: Draw a dense forest scene using layered color. Build depth by shifting colors cooler and lighter as the space recedes.
A wildflower field from close range — give individual flower heads precise attention while the background softens into color.
A coastal scene focusing on the color variation in rocks and tidal pools — the oranges, greens, greys, and purples of wet stone.
A sunset sky using warm layers burnished to achieve a smooth gradient from the horizon up.
An urban street focusing on the warm and cool variations in pavement, buildings, and shadow under direct sun.
Draw bare winter trees with detailed bark texture using layered warm and cool browns built slowly.
A garden scene — prioritize the subtle color variation of foliage over the flowers. Find the six greens in one single bush.
A mountain meadow in spring. Use the precision of colored pencil to study individual wildflowers against the broad landscape.
Both: A figure in a detailed landscape. Use the precision of the medium to give both the person and the setting equal, careful attention.
A person walking along a rainy city street. Capture the reflections on wet pavement with layered transparent glazing.
Two people at an outdoor café table. Give the tablecloth, the cups, the foliage, and the figures all the same level of specificity.
A fisherman in a small boat on still water — use the water reflections as an opportunity for layered color complexity.
A single figure reading in an autumn park. Match the warmth of the clothing to the surrounding warm tones of the leaves.
A market vendor surrounded by produce — use the saturated color of fruits and vegetables as the composition's engine.
A figure in a winter coat against a snowy urban background. Use cold light to push everything toward cool blue-grey.
A child in a garden — use the layering capacity of colored pencil to blend the warmth of the figure with the surrounding color.
Animals: Draw a bird with full feather detail — use layered color and careful hatching to describe iridescence and texture.
A cat's face with close attention to color variation in fur — warm tabby tones, cool grey shadows, bright highlights.
An insect in extreme close-up, as precise as a natural history illustration plate. Use the full capacity of the pencil.
A horse in full sun — focus on the warm and cool color variations across its coat in bright, direct light.
A dog's eye in close detail — use layering to build the depth and wetness of reflection in a single eye.
A butterfly with full wing detail — use the geometric precision of the wing pattern as your compositional anchor.
Draw a sleeping animal focusing on the color complexity of the fur or feathers without resorting to any outline.
A wild animal in its natural setting — prioritize the color relationship between creature and habitat.
Abstract: Layer complementary colors through cross-hatching until the optical mix creates a tone that is neither of the two source colors.
Create a composition using only the graduated pressure of a single pencil — from near-invisible to full saturation.
Build a color study using all warm pencils in one half of the composition and all cool pencils in the other. Find the border.
Explore burnishing: build up multiple layers to the point of wax bloom, then burnish back to a polished surface. Make this the subject.
Create a composition using only marks that move in one direction — diagonal, horizontal, or vertical. Switch only at transitions.
A composition that is entirely about surface: paper texture, wax bloom, soft marks, and hard marks together.
Layer every color available in your set in a single square inch. Study what happens to color when it fully saturates.
Work from a piece of music — translate its structure into a corresponding colored pencil composition.

People: Design a single-color linocut portrait using only the contrast of cut and uncut surface. Think entirely in positive and negative.
A figure in strong graphic silhouette — simplified to the minimum information a single block can carry.
A face of a crowd reduced to its essential graphic elements. Design the cut so white lines and black masses work as one system.
A single figure in three-quarter view. Use only large, decisive marks — nothing tentative survives the printing.
Design a linocut of hands at work. It should read as a bold graphic at distance and as careful observation up close.
A figure in motion — simplify the gesture to the boldest possible cuts. Think about what to remove, not what to add.
A portrait focused on one distinguishing feature. Use the rest of the face sparingly.
Design a figure that works as pure shape — white lines cutting through a black form to suggest volume.
Landscapes: Design a graphic landscape as a linocut. The sky, ground, and any elements should work as hard-edged shapes.
A seascape reduced to flat horizontal cuts — sky, horizon, sea, foam. Four zones, total.
A forest of bare trees designed for the block. Use the negative cuts to create light between the branches.
A rural landscape in black and white — bold sky, simple fields, clear horizon. Nothing more.
A mountain range designed as overlapping simplified silhouette shapes.
A city roofline at night — use bold black masses and white cut marks for lit windows.
A lone tree in a field under a large sky. Give three-quarters of the block to the sky and design it as carefully as the tree.
A coastal cliff face — use directional cuts to suggest rock texture without rendering it literally.
Both: A figure working in a landscape designed as a single bold graphic block.
A boat in a harbor — design the composition so boat and water read as interlocking positive and negative shapes.
A person on a coastal path — use the rhythm of the path cutting through the landscape as the composition's spine.
A market scene reduced to strong graphic shapes — figures, stalls, shadow areas working together.
A figure under a large tree — use the canopy as a framing element for the smaller figure below.
Two figures in silhouette on a ridge. Design their shapes so they read clearly against the sky.
A child playing on a beach — use the scale difference of child and sea to make the emotional point.
A farmer in a field — use bold repeated cuts for the furrows and keep the figure simple and strong.
Animals: Design a single animal in bold graphic reduction — the minimum marks needed for recognition and character.
A bird in flight as a linocut — simplify the wingspan to a clear graphic shape against an open sky.
A fish below a water surface — design the cut lines to suggest both the movement of the fish and the movement of water.
An animal portrait designed around a single strong gaze — use white cuts around the eye to create intensity.
A herd or flock reduced to repeating graphic shapes. Design the repetition as a composition.
A large animal seen from enough distance that its silhouette carries the whole story.
An insect or small creature designed as a precise graphic. Detailed cuts for the body; bold mass for the shadow.
A domestic animal in profile — find the five cuts that communicate it completely.
Abstract: Cut a pure pattern with no representational content. Think about how the cut marks create rhythm and movement.
Make a composition based entirely on the contrast between tight, dense cutting and broad open masses.
Design a block using only diagonal cuts moving in one direction. Add a second layer of cuts at ninety degrees.
Explore the linocut as a texture study — create three distinct texture zones and arrange them as a composition.
Design a block where the uncut black areas are as important as the cut white marks.
Make cuts of three different widths and design a composition using only those three mark weights.
Create a composition based on a simple grid. Cut marks into the grid in a pattern that creates something larger than the unit.
Explore repeat pattern design — a single motif that tiles or rotates into a full composition.

People: Draw a portrait using only flat color shapes with a broad-tip marker. Resist the urge to blend. Let the shapes do the work.
A figure in strong direct light — use a black marker for the shadow shapes and leave the light areas as bare white paper.
A crowd scene using only a black fine-tip marker and a single grey alcohol marker. Design the tones carefully with those two tools.
A face in profile using only line — vary the weight and see how much form one changing line can hold.
A quick portrait using a large brush marker. Work fast. Resist the urge to correct. Finish in under three minutes.
A figure in motion drawn with a single continuous line and a broad marker for the shadow mass.
A head-and-shoulders portrait using a fine-liner for structure and a brush marker for shadow fill.
Draw someone mid-gesture using only gesture lines — no interior detail permitted.
Landscapes: A city scene using only black line and flat grey fills. Design where the grey goes before you begin.
A landscape using a single broad marker in three different pressures to achieve three distinct values.
A tree-lined street using a fine-tip marker for architectural lines and a brush marker for foliage and shadows.
A seascape using only horizontal marker strokes — vary pressure and frequency to create both sky and sea.
A building facade in pure line with no tone or shadow. Make the lines carry all the architectural information.
A market or street scene using a palette of exactly three colored markers. Use each one purposefully.
Draw a park using only a fine black liner, leaving all light areas white. Work the darks and the voids.
An urban landscape at night — use dark markers and create the glow of streetlights through deliberate absence.
Both: A figure in a cityscape — use the boldest, flattest version of the scene a marker can produce.
A person at an outdoor café table in bold color. Let the marker bleed slightly and use that quality.
A child in a park drawn quickly with a brush marker. Work in ten minutes maximum. Accept what arrives.
A street scene with a single dominant figure — use color contrast to separate the figure from the ground.
A figure walking away on a path — use the broad marker shadow on the ground as the composition's anchor.
Two figures in conversation — draw their gestures only, no interior detail.
A market vendor seen across a crowd — use the flat abstract shapes of distance to construct the scene.
A person at a bus stop in rain — use loose, fast marks for the rain and careful shapes for the figure.
Animals: Draw a dog in bold flat color using a brush marker. Two colors plus paper white — nothing more.
A bird in simplified form — fine liner for the outline, broad marker for shadow and mass.
A cat drawn in a single continuous line. Close the loop at the end and see what you have.
An animal in motion using only gestural, overlapping marks.
A fish drawn using flat color blocks — scale pattern abstracted to pure graphic form.
A horse sketch in ten marks — broad, fast, committed. Do not correct.
An insect drawn with a fine liner in precise detail and shadowed with a broad marker loosely.
A wild animal at distance — use the flat markers to reduce it to a shape within its setting.
Abstract: Make a composition using five markers of varying widths and only horizontal marks. Do not plan it.
Create a color field composition — fill areas with flat color, leaving some white. Let the arrangement be the subject.
Work with overlapping translucent marker layers to see how the colors stack and interact.
Draw the same mark twenty times in slightly different ways with the same marker. See what variation produces.
A composition made entirely from circles drawn in three sizes and three colors.
Use a wet marker on slightly damp paper. Work with what the bleed produces, not against it.
Create a composition by drawing through the sheet — lay the paper over a textured surface and mark heavily.
A composition using only the dry, dragging end of a nearly-exhausted marker.

People: Build a portrait from torn magazine paper — no scissors. Use only the chance edges of torn paper for all shapes.
A figure constructed entirely from the text pages of a single newspaper. Let the type become texture and tone.
A portrait made from cut paper in a deliberately limited palette — three colors plus black and white.
Build a face from shapes that are not faces — cut color fields, abstract shapes, pattern fragments. Let the eye assemble it.
A figure in a setting built from the packaging and ephemera of a single day — receipts, wrappers, labels, tickets.
A self-portrait assembled from images that represent who you are rather than what you look like.
A figure in silhouette cut from a single piece of patterned paper, placed against a contrasting ground.
A figure assembled from photographs of hands, feet, and body parts cut from different sources. Make the dislocation deliberate.
Landscapes: A landscape built entirely from the color pages of magazines — use existing photography as abstract color material.
A cityscape using architectural photographs cut into geometric shapes and reassembled into something new.
A forest collage using green and brown paper torn into organic shapes and layered to suggest depth.
A sky assembled from blues, greys, and whites from different sources — create color harmony through careful curation.
A mountain landscape from cut paper in flat layers — the same approach as a woodblock print but in paper.
An urban scene built from cut maps, newspapers, and printed text. Let typography become part of the city.
An imaginary landscape assembled from elements that have never belonged together. Make it feel like a place.
A coastal scene using the actual color and texture of the source material to build sky, sea, and shore.
Both: A figure in a collaged landscape — use different source materials for the person and the setting so they are distinct but harmonious.
A city scene assembled from layers of cut and torn paper with one photographic figure placed into it.
A person standing in a garden — use seed packets, catalog pages, and printed imagery for the setting.
A person in a natural landscape where the human and natural elements are built from the same visual material — make them merge.
A child on a beach — use the actual paper textures of sand, water, and sky to build a world that feels physical.
A person reading surrounded by pages of text that function as both environment and interior life simultaneously.
A figure at a market built from the packaging, labels, and printed materials of actual products.
A traveler in a foreign landscape — find images of each in different sources and negotiate a new coherence.
Animals: Build an animal entirely from torn paper shapes — torn edges only, no scissors.
A bird collage using the colors and patterns of feathers sourced from natural history illustrations.
A fish assembled from blue and green color field fragments with pattern cut from different sources for the scales.
An animal portrait built from found photographs of unrelated subjects — source all your colors from reality.
A wild animal assembled from the landscape it lives in — use the same visual material for both creature and habitat.
An insect built from technical diagrams, natural history illustration, and abstract color field.
A series of small animal silhouettes, each filled with a different paper pattern.
A pet portrait built from objects and packaging associated with that animal — make the materials tell a story.
Abstract: Create a grid of torn paper squares in twelve different textures. Arrange them as a pure composition.
Build a composition using only the white space between deliberately placed cut elements.
Use nothing but text — cut from books, newspapers, and packaging — as your visual material. Text becomes pure form.
Assemble a composition based on a single color family. Find every version of it across different sources.
Create a composition by overlapping translucent tissue paper in multiple layers. Let the layering create the image.
Make a composition using found ephemera from a single day — tickets, wrappers, receipts, handwriting.
Tear paper into strips and weave or braid them into a composition.
Work completely without scissors — fold, tear, layer, and arrange until the composition is complete.
